upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows a very weak lapse rate of 0.40°C/km from surface to 850mb — essentially isothermal/stable, far below the dry adiabatic rate of 9.8°C/km — indicating a strong inversion capping convective development. The 850mb winds are light at 21 kts from 005°, turning to calm at 700mb and then 27 kts from 325° at 500mb, suggesting moderate wind shear aloft. Thermal ceiling today is effectively limited to ~500m AGL (BL depths 400–550m by early afternoon), constraining soaring altitude significantly.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the Bay Area SF region. KHAF showing NW winds 13–15 kts gusting 17 kts late afternoon, consistent with strengthening sea breeze. KAPC reporting NE winds 11G19 kts — notable gradient suggesting active pressure-driven flow. KRHV clear with light W-SW 6–8 kts by late afternoon, confirming valley sea breeze arrival. No significant precipitation, icing, or turbulence advisories in effect.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — inland thermal/ridge site, NE-facing launch, SE to NW valley breeze cycle
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin 1750 works on thermal lift with valley breeze support. Ideal conditions are light-to-moderate NW or W winds at launch (8–15 kts), good surface heating, and BL heights above 600m. The high launch extends soaring range significantly when thermals are firing.
sites.todaySetup: Today's dominant story at Ed Levin 1750 is excess wind during the morning and the very shallow BL capping thermal development all day. The biggest issue early is the NE surface flow running 14–17 kts at 10m with 80m winds an alarming 21–24 kts — this is a strong, shear-heavy NE gradient flow that makes launch conditions punchy and turbulent before 11 AM. Wind direction is unfavorable (NE/ENE, 41–49°) for this NW-facing launch throughout the morning and midday. BL tops stay under 500m until mid-afternoon, limiting any thermal development. By afternoon (2–5 PM) winds ease to 10–12 kts from a more variable NNE–N direction and BL climbs to ~480–550m — the best window of the day, but still a modest thermal environment. CAPE is effectively zero all day. Tomorrow.io data is for Apr 4, not today, so today's picture is driven by HRRR/GFS which are in perfect agreement.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: NE winds 13–17 kts at surface, 22–24 kts at 80m — strong shear and unfavorable direction for launch. BL only 210–385m. Avoid.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds easing slightly to 12–15 kts NE, BL rising to 425–445m. Direction still unfavorable; conditions marginal at best and wind shear aloft remains concerning.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window: winds drop to 10–12 kts trending more N/NNE, BL 440–495m. Still modest thermals and questionable direction, but the most manageable period of the day.

verdict.hazards: Strong shear morning (80m winds 22–24 kts), unfavorable NE direction at launch, very shallow BL under 500m all day, wind eases but direction questionable for site mechanics

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — lower training hill, NE-facing, sheltered valley launch
sites.howItWorks: The 600 ft training hill at Ed Levin works best in light-moderate W to NW winds, suited to beginner and supervised P2 flights. It is much more exposed to surface flow variations and offers limited soaring potential compared to the high launch.
sites.todaySetup: The 600 ft launch is essentially unusable for quality flights today due to the same NE gradient flow hammering the site. Morning winds at 10m are 19–24 kts from NNE (31–42°) — well above P2 limits and in a cross/unfavorable direction. The 80m layer is running 25–30 kts in the early morning, indicating serious wind shear in the lower atmosphere. Even though winds ease to 10–13 kts by afternoon, the direction (NNE/N) is still marginal for this launch orientation, and BL tops of 535–555m offer only modest soaring if direction were ideal. The lower launch is the wrong place to be in a NE gradient flow day.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 19–24 kts from NNE at surface, 25–30 kts at 80m — dangerously strong and shear-loaded. No-fly.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds easing to 16–17 kts NE at surface, still strong. BL 370–510m. Conditions remain above safe P2 limits.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds down to 11–13 kts from NNE; most manageable period but direction remains off for this launch. P3+ only with caution.

verdict.hazards: Excessive morning winds 19–24 kts, severe 80m shear up to 30 kts, unfavorable NE direction all day, not appropriate for training hill operations

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal ridge, W to NW-facing, ocean ridge soaring site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a ridge soaring site powered by the marine sea breeze and onshore NW-W flow. Ideal winds are 10–20 kts from 270–315°. The site needs enough onshore flow to sustain ridge lift but not so strong that launches become dangerous or rotor develops below the ridge.
sites.todaySetup: Mussel Rock has a split personality today — the morning is dead calm and unrideable, but the afternoon develops into the day's best opportunity. Early morning surface winds are near calm (1–3 kts) from the east, meaning zero ridge lift. The site begins to wake up around 11 AM as NE-to-E flow shifts toward more onshore directions, but the critical transition happens in the afternoon. By 3–4 PM, winds shift NW (316–336°) at 6–8 kts and the 80m layer shows 8–9 kts — this is the classic sea breeze ridge soaring window. KHAF METARs confirm NW 13–15 kts gusting 17 kts at Half Moon Bay by late afternoon, and the SF Bar buoy shows W-NW 9.7 kts. SST at 12.6–12.7°C is cold, which deepens the marine layer but also means the sea breeze gradient is robust. No marine layer obscuring the ridge today (KHAF CLR, 10SM visibility). The morning transition period (10 AM–noon) with variable ENE flow and BL heights reaching 480–880m is the wildcard — direction is highly variable and unsuitable for consistent ridge lift.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Near-calm to light E winds 1–8 kts — no onshore flow, no ridge lift. Site is unflying.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Variable flow from ENE to E, 6–8 kts — transitional, inconsistent direction. Not reliable for ridge soaring; occasional pockets of lift possible but not sustaining.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window: NW shift arrives 3–4 PM, winds 6–8 kts from 315–336° at surface, 7–10 kts at 80m. KHAF confirming NW 13 kts. Classic ridge soaring conditions; sustained lift likely along the ridge face.

verdict.hazards: Morning dead calm unusable; variable transitional flow midday; afternoon window solid but brief; cold SST (12.6°C) means marine layer could deepen quickly near sunset

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont — inland thermal site, W-facing launch, East Bay hills
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak soars on thermals generated by inland surface heating, with the valley sea breeze from the bay providing a reliable trigger and face lift. Ideal conditions are light-to-moderate W/NW winds 8–18 kts at launch and BL heights above 600m for good soaring altitude.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak shares the same problematic NE gradient flow as Ed Levin today. Morning winds of 13–17 kts from NE/ENE (41–49°) are directly opposing the W-facing launch, creating headwind-reverse conditions that are unsafe. The 80m winds of 22–24 kts compound the problem with serious low-level shear. BL heights are capped at 385–445m through midday, limiting thermal development even if the flow were favorable. The afternoon sees winds ease to 10–12 kts and direction edge toward more N/NNE — still cross or opposing for a W-facing launch. KRHV shows W-SW 6–8 kts by late afternoon, suggesting the sea breeze is beginning to win in the valley below, but the hilltop flow lags. The shallow BL (under 500m all day) and unfavorable NE flow combine to make this a marginal-to-no-fly day at Mission Peak.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: NE winds 13–17 kts at launch face, 22–24 kts at 80m — opposing the W-facing launch and excessively shear-laden. No-fly.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds ease slightly to 12–15 kts still from NE. BL 385–445m. Wrong direction for Mission Peak mechanics; marginal at best.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 10–12 kts trending more N, BL 440–495m. KRHV showing sea breeze arriving in valley below. Closest to flyable, but launch direction remains problematic; P3+ experienced locals only.

verdict.hazards: NE gradient opposes W-facing launch all day, strong 80m shear morning (22–24 kts), BL capped under 500m, wind direction incompatible with site mechanics until late afternoon

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Saturday April 4 shows a dramatic reversal — winds drop to near-calm (2–5 kts) all day but 100% cloud cover persists from morning through evening per both HRRR and GFS in perfect agreement. BL climbs to an impressive 1590–1765m by afternoon suggesting strong heating potential beneath the cloud deck, but with overcast skies, thermals will be suppressed. Tomorrow.io confirms calm winds (1–2 kts) but also shows 76–100% cloud cover afternoon. An overcast, calm day — not a soaring day.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Same story as the high launch — calm winds (2–5 kts) all day with complete overcast. BL heights are theoretically high (1335–1870m) suggesting instability under the cloud deck, but without solar heating penetrating to the surface, thermals will be absent. Not suitable for meaningful flights.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Saturday looks challenging for Mussel Rock. Morning shows ESE winds 8–11 kts — onshore but in the wrong direction (E, not NW). By afternoon, GFS shows winds going nearly calm (0.4 kts) at 2 PM then shifting W-NW 4–5 kts by 4–6 PM. Tomorrow.io disagrees significantly, showing E-SE 8–10 kts gusting 11–14 kts through the day — potential for a messy cross/side-shore flow. 100% cloud cover all day. Weighting Tomorrow.io more heavily for the easterly surface flow, this looks like an unfavorable direction day with overcast skies and no clear sea breeze window.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Saturday April 4 is calm and overcast — winds 2–5 kts variable with 100% cloud cover all day. The afternoon BL of 1590m suggests the atmosphere wants to mix but cloud cover prevents surface heating from driving thermals. Tomorrow.io shows light SE-E 2–6 kts gusting 7 kts — not enough for ridge lift and wrong direction for thermals. No viable flying window expected.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Sunday April 5 looks more promising. Cloud cover clears by late morning, BL climbs to 815–1415m by noon–2 PM, and W-NW winds of 5–9 kts develop from 290–325° — much more favorable direction and speed for thermal flying. Afternoon could offer genuine soaring with moderate thermals. Watch the afternoon cloud redevelopment (73% by 4 PM) which may cut thermal production early.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Sunday April 5 mirrors the high launch with clearer skies midday, BL up to 815–1415m, and W-NW winds 5–9 kts. Good potential for thermal flying in the 11 AM–3 PM window before afternoon clouds build. Better than Saturday by a wide margin.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Sunday shows variable/light winds early (2–4 kts from mixed directions) clearing to W-NW 6–9 kts by afternoon with BL heights of 930–1400m — this is much more promising ridge lift potential. However 98% cloud cover returns by 4 PM. The midday window around noon–2 PM with clear skies and 6 kts W-NW could be a solid ridge session.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Sunday April 5 has potential — clear skies midday, BL climbing to 910–1605m, and W-NW winds building to 6–12 kts at 80m by afternoon. Surface winds remain light (3–6 kts) which is typical for thermal days at Mission Peak. Could be the best inland soaring day of the forecast period if the afternoon clouds (13% by 4 PM) stay limited.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: No data beyond April 5 in the current forecast window. Trend from Saturday (overcast/calm) through Sunday (improving/thermal) suggests a building ridge pattern — Monday may continue improving if the NW flow establishes more firmly.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Same pattern as high launch — insufficient data for Day 3 but trend suggests continued improvement into next week if the ridging pattern holds.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Insufficient data for Day 3 (beyond April 5). If Sunday's NW flow pattern strengthens, Monday could bring excellent ridge soaring conditions at Mussel Rock.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Day 3 beyond current forecast window. The improving trend through Sunday suggests continued NW flow pattern; watch for classic inland thermal days developing if the marine influence stays moderate.

watchlist: Today's NE gradient flow (80m winds 22–24 kts) creates serious low-level shear at all inland sites — do not underestimate based on surface readings alone
KAPC reporting NE 11G19 kts confirms a real pressure-driven NE gradient, not just model noise — adds weight to the morning no-fly recommendation at Ed Levin and Mission Peak

Cold SST 12.6–12.7°C at both Half Moon Bay and SF Bar buoys with 12-second wave period indicates a deep, persistent marine layer — monitor Saturday afternoon for possible delayed sea breeze at Mussel Rock
Saturday April 4 overcast (100% cloud cover, both models agree) will kill thermals despite impressively deep BL of 1590–1870m — do not be fooled by the BL height numbers
Sunday April 5 afternoon cloud redevelopment (73% by 4 PM at Ed Levin) could cut the thermal window short — aim for early afternoon flights that day
tldr: Today is mostly a bust for inland sites — a NE gradient flow is pumping 22–24 kts at 80m into the wrong direction at Ed Levin and Mission Peak all morning, with a weak BL capping thermals under 500m. Mussel Rock is your best bet today but only in the late afternoon (3–5:30 PM) when the NW sea breeze finally kicks in. Weekend outlook: Saturday is overcast and calm across all sites (skip it), Sunday looks like the real soaring day with clear skies, BL up to 1400m, and NW winds arriving at Ed Levin and Mission Peak.
